name: Scandinavia
description: The Nordic peninsula and Danish lowlands — modern Norway, Sweden, and Denmark. Homeland of the Norse peoples; origin of the Viking expansion.

periods.note: Germanic Nordic peoples — ancestors of the Norse; Germanic Iron Age culture; trade networks with Rome (amber route); runic writing from ~200 CE. Distinct tribal groups: Danes (south), Svear and Götar (Sweden), Norwegians (west). Germanic mythology (Odin, Thor, the Nine Worlds) fully developed by this period.

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant — Proto-Norse then Old Norse linguistic community
minority hunter-gatherers in the far north — separate culture maintained

periods.note: Viking Age and Christian kingdoms — raid on Lindisfarne (793) marks the opening of the Viking Age. Norwegian, Danish, and Swedish kingdoms emerged and competed. Norway unified by Harald Fairhair c.872. Christianisation: Olaf I (Norway, 994–1000), Olaf II/Saint Olaf (1015–1028). Cnut's North Sea Empire briefly unified England, Denmark, Norway (1016–1035). Iceland settled 874; Norse reached Greenland 985 and North America c.1000.

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant — Norse culture at its peak geographical reach

periods.note: Danish kingdom — the dominant Scandinavian power of the early medieval period; controlled access to the Baltic through the Øresund strait. Harald Bluetooth unified Denmark and Christianised it (~965). Cnut's empire (1016–1035) included England. Valdemar II expanded into the Baltic (13th century).

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant in Denmark; controlling Baltic trade

periods.note: Swedish kingdom — the Svear and Götar united into one kingdom under the Eriksson dynasty (~970). Christianisation 11th century. Sweden's wealth came from iron and furs. Birger Jarl (1210–1266) strengthened royal authority and founded Stockholm. Swedish expansion into Finland began 12th century.

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant — Swedish-speaking majority; Finnish-speaking minority in east

periods.note: Post-Kalmar Denmark-Norway — Sweden broke away 1523 under Gustav Vasa; Denmark retained Norway and Denmark proper. Lutheranism became state religion 1536. Denmark-Norway was the dominant regional power until the Great Northern War; lost southern Sweden (Scania, Blekinge) to Sweden at Roskilde (1658). Norway ceded to Sweden in 1814 after Napoleon's defeat.

Swedish independence and Empire — Gustav Vasa broke from Kalmar Union; Lutheran Reformation. Under Gustav II Adolf (1611–1632) Sweden became a European Great Power (Stormaktstiden), controlling the Baltic rim. Declined after Charles XII's defeat at Poltava (1709); lost Finland to Russia 1809. Constitutionally transformed into parliamentary democracy in the 20th century.

ethnic_composition.presence: indigenous minority in the north; increasingly marginalised

periods.note: Modern Denmark — lost Norway to Sweden (1814) and Schleswig-Holstein to Prussia (1864); Denmark became a small, compact state and compensated through social development, becoming one of the world's most advanced welfare states by the 20th century.

Norway in Swedish union (1814–1905) — forced into personal union with Sweden after Napoleonic era. Norwegian national consciousness intensified; Norway dissolved the union peacefully in 1905 and became fully independent.
Kingdom of Norway (independent) — chose Haakon VII as king (a Danish prince by request). Occupied by Nazi Germany 1940–1945; post-war became one of the world's wealthiest nations on North Sea oil.
